topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room hummed, a grating sound that seemed to amplify the silence between me and Coach Miller. I traced the worn grain of the wooden bench with my fingertips, the wood cool beneath my touch. Every time he cleared his throat, the sound felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest. I kept my gaze fixed on my worn sneakers, refusing to meet his eyes, the familiar tightening in my throat a constant companion. When he finally spoke, his voice was low, measured, and utterly final. "This roster needs to be streamlined," he stated, his tone devoid of warmth. The air in the room seemed to thicken, making every breath a conscious effort. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a cold, hollow sensation spreading through my limbs. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, the taste of ash suddenly bitter on my tongue.

[story 2]

I replayed the final practice in my mind, the missed passes, the clumsy tackles—each failure a sharp, stinging reminder of my inadequacy. When Coach called me into his office, the silence was oppressive, heavy enough to choke on. I sat rigidly in the hard plastic chair, my hands clasped so tightly my knuckles were white islands against my skin. He began speaking, his words precise and surgical, dissecting my performance with an unnerving clarity. I watched his expression—a mask of professional detachment—and felt a profound sense of unraveling. When he delivered the news, it wasn't a shout or an outburst; it was a quiet pronouncement, a closing of a door I hadn't realized was locked. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscle vibrating beneath my skin. I didn't speak; I just stared at the scuffed linoleum floor, willing myself to disappear.

[story 3]

The weight of the team's expectations felt like a physical burden, crushing my shoulders down toward my ears. I kept glancing toward the doorway, anticipating the moment the inevitable conversation would begin. When Coach finally pulled me aside, the casual way he addressed me felt like a deliberate act of erasure. He didn't waste time on pleasantries. His voice was flat, carrying an undeniable finality that settled deep in my gut like a stone. "We're making some changes," he said, gesturing vaguely toward the roster sheet lying on the desk. A strange numbness washed over me, a complete shutdown of feeling. I managed a weak, "Understood," the words scraping out of my throat like dry sand. Every muscle in my body felt locked, unable to move, as if I were submerged in thick, cold water.
